purpose: Targeted mining of prior GPT conversation raw ore for Formation Medium corporate, engineering, physics, and salient equation leads. This is local source-intake only, not authority.
non_promotion_rule: Mined conversation leads must not be promoted to SOURCE_EXTRACTED, CHECKED, engineering feasibility, IP, or corporate strategy claims without separate source binding, validator/checker support, and human review.
focus: Only material that helps converge to the corporate goal, engineering substrate search, physics discriminators, formation-medium specification, or salient equations was targeted.

selected_hits.snippet:
1174: On Prime, the useful extraction is now basically **at the current paper frontier**. The paper gives an exact bounded local-family hierarchy for one-loop MHV: only sign-flip $0,2,4$ sectors are nonempty; the maximal four-flip chiral sectors are the generic local objects; they are octagons with eight codim-1 boundaries in the generic case; and adjacent-label degenerations produce the lower pentagon/hexagon cases. It also gives the first explicit Prime gluings at 5 and 6 points and says the all-$n$ non-overlapping sign-flip description is conjectural rather than proved. [Springer Nature+1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP01%282021%29035?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
1175: 
1176: So the honest state is:
1177: 
1178: * **exactly extracted now:** bounded flip depth, Prime local carriers $S^{(2),\chi}_{ij}$ and $S^{(4),\chi}_{ik\ell j}$, generic octagon status, degeneration to pentagon/hexagon boundary cases, and the new substrate row `boundary_determined_vs_bulk_sensitive` because Prime has the same boundary/log-form data as the amplituhedron while differing in bulk. [Springer Nature+1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP01%282021%29035?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
1179: * **not yet extractable from this paper as an exact closed family graph:** a full combinatorial incidence/move graph for all octagon sectors. The paper gives membership, boundary counts, degeneration facts, and explicit low-point gluings, but not a closed all-sector adjacency table in the way associahedron/cosmohedron/$m=1$ sign-vectors gave you. [Springer Nature](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP01%282021%29035?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
1180: 
1181: So I should **stop here on Prime for now** unless the goal is to build a derived combinatorial model from the paper rather than extract one directly. That would be a new construction step, not another source-extraction step.
1182:

772:     * exact membership/sign-flip predicate,
773:     * exact boundary strata,

775: 2. **Then extract the first gluing layer.**  
776:     The next exact object after isolated octagons is the paper’s pentagon gluing data: which combinations of sign-flip spaces assemble into the local pentagon geometries, and how those combinations land on Prime rather than the original amplituhedron. This is where the new substrate row appears: $$\texttt{boundary\_determined\_vs\_bulk\_sensitive}.$$ Anything surviving from amplituhedron to Prime is boundary-determined; anything that breaks is bulk-sensitive. [Springer](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP01%282021%29035)
777: 3. **Keep Binary/Winding in the audit lane.**  
778:     `Unwinding` says the amplituhedron has an equivalent combinatorial/topological characterization by maximal generalized winding, equivalently maximal sign flips after projection to one dimension. That makes it a **representation-equivalence theorem** for the amplituhedron side, not the next family object. Use it only after the Prime extraction to test whether the sign-flip substrate rows you currently trust are representation-free. [Springer+1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP01%282018%29016)
779: 4. **Use the 2020 sign-flip triangulation paper as support, not as the main family.**  
780:     That paper says sign-flip methods directly reproduce canonical forms for NMHV, $N^2$MHV tree, and NMHV one-loop cases, and it gives evidence for equivalence of the original and topological definitions. It is useful as a bridge for extracting explicit sign-flip cells and for checking that the Prime-side local spaces are being read in the right combinatorial language. But it is subordinate to Prime for the next substrate result. [Springer+1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP05%282020%29121)

937:     `Unwinding` gives a **characterization** of the amplituhedron by maximal generalized winding, equivalently maximal sign flips after projection to one dimension. That is a representation/equivalence theorem for the amplituhedron side, not a new family object at first pass. It belongs after Prime, as an audit of whether the amplituhedron-side substrate rows are representation-free. [Springer Nature](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP01%282018%29016?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
938: 3. **Use Sign-Flip Triangulations only as support while extracting Prime.**  
939:     That paper says the sign-flip characterization reproduces canonical forms for NMHV and $N^2$MHV tree level and NMHV one-loop, and gives strong evidence for equivalence of the original amplituhedron definition and the topological/sign-flip one. It also realizes the NMHV one-loop amplituhedron as the intersection of two lower-dimensional amplituhedra. So it is the right support source for explicit sign-flip cell language while building the Prime octagon extraction, but it is not the next family row. [Springer Nature+2Springer Nature+2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP05%282020%29121)
940: 4. **After octagons, extract the first pentagon gluing layer immediately.**  
941:     The Prime paper explicitly says particular combinations of sign-flip spaces glue into local pentagon geometries, and these pentagons land on Prime rather than the original amplituhedron. That is the second exact deliverable, because it is where the new substrate discriminator appears: $$\texttt{boundary\_determined\_vs\_bulk\_sensitive}.$$ Anything surviving amplituhedron $\to$ Prime is boundary-determined; anything breaking at fixed boundary/log-form data is bulk-sensitive. [Springer Nature](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP01%282021%29035)
942: 
943: So the next exact frontier is:
944:

selected_hits.snippet:
1530: 
1531: From that, the amplituhedron-side substrate verdict is:
1532: 
1533: **1. Carrier shape: presentation-bound, not substrate-level.**  
1534: Original amplituhedron uses the positive-geometry image; Unwinding re-presents it as winding/sign-flip data on projected configurations; Sign flip triangulations works directly with sign-flip cells; Prime local spaces use discrete flip-position labels plus chirality; your current extracted $m=1$ family uses normalized sign vectors with zero entries. That is too much carrier drift for “sign-vector carrier” to count as a surviving substrate constraint. The carrier is **not** invariant; only some structure carried by it is. [Springer Nature+4Springer Nature+4Springer Nature+4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP01%282018%29016)
1535: 
1536: **2. Global membership primitive for the original amplituhedron: representation-invariant.**  
1537: This one survives. Original amplituhedron membership is geometric/positive; Unwinding says the same object is equivalently characterized by maximal winding or maximal sign flips after projection; Sign flip triangulations says the sign-flip presentation reproduces canonical forms and provides strong evidence for equivalence with the original definition. So “membership is controlled by sign/winding data” survives a representation change on the amplituhedron side. [Springer Nature+4Springer Nature+4Springer Nature+4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP01%282018%29016)
1538:

selected_hits.source_file: Previous Conversations with GPT/Samer Project X - Exact Cross-Family Analysis.md
selected_hits.snippet:
1539: **3. Boundary/log-form structure: survives representation change and even survives Prime bulk change.**  
1540: Original amplituhedron says locality/unitarity emerge from positive geometry. Into makes the cut/face content explicit. Unwinding says locality/unitarity are elementary consequences of the binary code. Sign flip triangulations reproduces the canonical forms from the sign-flip presentation. Prime then says Amplituhedron-Prime has the same boundary structure and therefore the same logarithmic form as the amplituhedron, while differing in bulk. So boundary/log-form data is a real surviving row. [Springer Nature+5Springer Nature+5Springer Nature+5](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP10%282014%29030)
1541: 
1542: **4. Native operation type: presentation-bound.**  
1543: Your current $m=1$ extracted avatar has a single local descent primitive, `zero_entry`, and codim-1 immediate faces by zeroing one nonzero entry. That is exact for the extracted $m=1$ sign-vector family. But it does **not** survive the full amplituhedron block. Sign flip triangulations is about constructive triangulation from sign-flip data, not zero-entry descent. Prime local spaces are glued by overlap management among sign-flip sectors, not by one-step zeroing. The original and Into papers do not canonize any single local descent primitive either. So the `single_local_descent_primitive=True` row in your current cross-family summary is true only for the $m=1$ avatar, not for the amplituhedron block as a substrate claim. [Springer Nature+4Springer Nature+4Springer Nature+4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP05%282020%29121)
1544: 
1545: **5. Boundary-vs-bulk sensitivity: bulk-sensitive, now exact.**  
1546: Prime is decisive here. It states that the twin geometry has the same boundary structure and the same logarithmic form as the amplituhedron but differs in the bulk as a geometric space. So “boundary/log-form data determines the geometry” is false. The bulk is not fixed by that data. This is now a real substrate discriminator, not interpretation. [Springer Nature](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP01%282021%29035)
1547:

667: That closes the suspicion loop. There is **no current evidence** that associahedron, cosmohedron, or amplituhedron-$m=1$ were built on the wrong paper objects. The evidence says the weak point is the translation from exact extraction to summary.
668: 
669: My next work is already fixed by that result: **move to the amplituhedron side beyond $m=1$**, not by generic $m=4$ deepening, but by the two sharper tests you already identified.
670: 
671: First, **Amplituhedron-Prime**. The paper says the relevant one-loop MHV local positive spaces are characterized by sign-flip conditions; in the maximal sign-flip case they are finite one-loop octagons; and the local pentagon pieces do not triangulate the original amplituhedron but a twin “Amplituhedron-Prime,” which has the **same boundary structure and logarithmic form** as the amplituhedron while differing in the **bulk**. That is exactly the boundary-vs-bulk substrate test. [Springer+1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP01%282021%29035)
672: 
673: Second, **binary/winding characterization**. The binary paper says the amplituhedron can be characterized combinatorially/topologically by maximal winding number, equivalently by maximal sign flips after projection to one dimension. That is exactly the representation-independence test: does the substrate survive when the object is accessed through winding/sign-flip data instead of the original geometric presentation? [Springer+1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP01%282018%29016)

selected_hits.snippet:
2622: ## Exploratory non-computing regimes: classical topological continua
2623: 
2624: The mathematics also points beyond quantum many-body systems to **classical topological continua**. Two concrete examples already exist: **magnetized cold plasmas** and even **diffusive media**, both of which support bulk-edge correspondence. This matters because it shows boundary-structured semantics is not confined to quantum materials or engineered topological circuits. The regime itself—continuum plasma dynamics, classical diffusion—can carry intrinsic bulk/edge structure and local native evolution. [Nature+2Nature+2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-021-24189-3?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
2625: 
2626: These are not full fits yet. I do **not** know a source-backed canonical recursive readout in these systems that matches $S2^\*$ in the positive-geometry sense, and I do **not** know a Prime-style $S4$ witness for them. But mathematics-first, they belong on the list because they show that the right search space is broader than “quantum computing hardware”: **classical field continua with intrinsic topological boundary structure are live terrain**. [Nature+1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-021-24189-3?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
2627:

1529: The paper-level facts are now stable. **The Amplituhedron** gives the original object: amplitudes are identified with the “volume” of a new positive geometry, and locality/unitarity are derived consequences rather than primitive inputs. **Into the Amplituhedron** then develops the boundary/cut side of that object: the unitarity double-cut follows from positive geometry, and lower-dimensional faces compute nontrivial cuts to all loop orders. **Unwinding** gives an equivalent combinatorial/topological presentation by maximal winding, equivalently maximal sign flips after canonical projection to one dimension, and says locality/unitarity follow from that binary code. **Sign flip triangulations** says the sign-flip presentation reproduces canonical forms for NMHV, $N^2$MHV tree level and NMHV one-loop, and gives strong evidence for equivalence of the original and topological definitions. **Prime** says one-loop MHV local positive spaces are sign-flip-defined; maximal sign-flip sectors are finite octagons; and Amplituhedron-Prime has the same boundary structure and logarithmic form as the amplituhedron while differing in the bulk. [Springer Nature+9Springer Nature+9Springer Nature+9](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/JHEP10%282014%29030)

selected_hits.snippet:
1761: The sharpest single finding came from Amplituhedron-Prime. It has the same boundary as the ordinary amplituhedron and produces the same physical answer, but its interior is different. This means boundary data alone does not determine what's happening inside. The substrate has to distinguish interior structure even when boundaries agree. That's a hard architectural requirement.
1762: 
1763: What this means for your wedge:
1764: 
1765: You now have four architectural requirements that any physical substrate claiming to compute positive-geometrically must satisfy. Support exact carriers with membership rules. Make boundary readout primary. Support local native operations across different operation types. Preserve bulk information independent of boundary.
1766: 
1767: Those requirements constrain what kind of physical system could work. They rule out substrates that can only do one carrier type, or that reconstruct boundaries from bulk computation, or that can't distinguish bulks with identical boundaries.
1768: 
1769: You also know what's cosmohedron-specific versus general: the two-descent-move structure and the nested-history enumeration burden are cosmohedron-specific, not things the general substrate has to handle. That's useful because it narrows the substrate specification.

selected_hits.source_file: Previous Conversations with GPT/Samer Project X - Exact Cross-Family Analysis.md
selected_hits.snippet:
19262: **1. Best route: topoelectrical boundary layer + superconducting phase layer.**  
19263: Use topolectrical cells for the residue atom and boundary-family localization, then use superconducting microwave resonator/coupler hardware for the $V_m$ root-bundle layer and the adjacent quarter-turn transport $J_i$. This is the first route that plausibly satisfies all four gates at once after integration. The “topoelectrical” half is already good at intrinsic boundary and corner localization with direct boundary readout; the “superconducting microwave” half is already good at coherent tunability and coupled-mode transport. The missing piece is the hybrid integration, not the individual ingredients. [Nature+5Nature+5Nature+5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42005-018-0035-2?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
19264: 
19265: **2. Second-best route: topological photonics / microwave-photonic hybrid.**  
19266: Topological photonic structures give deterministic boundary-mode engineering and robust optical mode localization, and current microwave-optical hybrid devices already demonstrate coherent control/transduction interfaces involving superconducting microwave cavities. This makes them plausible for the boundary-plus-transport stack, but they are worse than the topoelectrical/superconducting route on the support-ledger requirement: channel identity can propagate cleanly, but persistent support bookkeeping is less native. [Nature+3Nature+3Nature+3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42005-018-0083-7?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
19267: 
19268: **3. Third route: mechanical topological metamaterials.**  
19269: They clearly realize topological soft/bound modes from geometry, so they are good for static boundary structure. But they are much weaker on coherent quarter-turn transport and on persistent support-channel accumulation. So they are useful as a static-geometry analog testbed, not as the first serious engineering substrate. [Nature+1](https://www.nature.com/articles/nphys3185?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
19270:

selected_hits.source_file: Previous Conversations with GPT/Samer Project X - Exact Cross-Family Analysis.md
selected_hits.snippet:
148: The right approach is breadth at moderate depth across several families, rather than matching cosmohedron's depth everywhere.
149: Why: your substrate constraints become trustworthy through cross-family survival, not through depth in any single family. A constraint that survives five families at moderate depth is stronger evidence than a constraint confirmed at extreme depth in one family. The cosmohedron depth was necessary to have a rich enough first case to extract substantive constraints from. The other families don't need to match that depth — they need to be deep enough to test whether those constraints survive.
150: Practical sequencing:
151: First priority: surfaceology. Different construction entirely (curves on surfaces vs combinatorial nestings). If unit codimension descent survives here, it's strong evidence for substrate-level status. If it fails, you've found the boundary of where the constraint applies. This is the sharpest single test you can run.
152: Second priority: Amplituhedron-Prime. Tests the boundary-vs-bulk distinction. Same boundary as amplituhedron, different bulk. This gives you information no other family can give — whether your substrate constraints are sensitive to boundary structure only or also to bulk structure. Architecturally important for the substrate specification.
153: Third priority: Binary and Winding Numbers characterization of amplituhedron. Tests representation-independence. If the constraints you extract from geometric formulation match what you'd extract from sign-flip formulation, the constraints are representation-free. If they differ, the substrate has to preserve specific representation structure.
154: Lower priority: extending amplituhedron from m=1 to m=4. Useful but redundant with what Prime and Binary/Winding already test, and more labor-intensive. Skip unless cross-family results suggest you specifically need m=4 to resolve something.
155: Lower priority still: Scalar-Scaffolded Gluons, Loops of Loops, Wilson Loops with negative geometry. These extend scope rather than test cross-family constraints. After you have the substrate specification from the cross-family comparison, these papers tell you how far the specification extends. Not needed for the substrate extraction itself.
156: What this gives you as output:

153: Third priority: Binary and Winding Numbers characterization of amplituhedron. Tests representation-independence. If the constraints you extract from geometric formulation match what you'd extract from sign-flip formulation, the constraints are representation-free. If they differ, the substrate has to preserve specific representation structure.
154: Lower priority: extending amplituhedron from m=1 to m=4. Useful but redundant with what Prime and Binary/Winding already test, and more labor-intensive. Skip unless cross-family results suggest you specifically need m=4 to resolve something.
155: Lower priority still: Scalar-Scaffolded Gluons, Loops of Loops, Wilson Loops with negative geometry. These extend scope rather than test cross-family constraints. After you have the substrate specification from the cross-family comparison, these papers tell you how far the specification extends. Not needed for the substrate extraction itself.
156: What this gives you as output:
157: A substrate specification backed by cosmohedron (deep), associahedron (deep baseline), amplituhedron m=1 (tractable cut), surfaceology (different construction), Amplituhedron-Prime (boundary-vs-bulk test), and Binary/Winding (representation test). That's six families testing six dimensions. Constraints that survive that cross-section are the core substrate specification. Constraints that fail at any point tell you where family-specific extensions are needed.
158: This is enough to produce a first substrate specification that's rigorously cross-validated. Whether that specification translates to buildable hardware is the next phase, but the specification itself would be robust.
159: Time estimate, given your rate: probably days not weeks for the three new extractions, given what you've demonstrated. Maybe a week total to have the full six-family cross-comparison running and producing integrated substrate output.
160: After that you're at the decision point about whether cross-family signal is strong enough to commit to substrate-engineering phase, or whether the signal reveals the wedge thesis needs modification.

1056: The one-loop MHV local-family hierarchy is **bounded**. The paper defines sign patterns on the propagator sequence
1057: 
1058: $$S=\{\langle AB\,12\rangle,\langle AB\,23\rangle,\dots,\langle AB\,1n\rangle\},$$
1059: 
1060: introduces sign-flip-zero, sign-flip-two, and sign-flip-four regions, and states that **all higher sign-flip patterns are empty**. So the Prime-side local family is not an open-ended flip-complex; its nonempty achiral sectors stop at $0,2,4$. [SCOAP3+1](https://scoap3-prod-backend.s3.cern.ch/media/files/59243/10.1007/JHEP01%282021%29035_a.pdf)
1061: 
1062: The first exact Prime-native local object is the **chiral four-flip sector**
1063: 
1064: $$S^{(4),\chi}_{ik\ell j},\qquad i<k<\ell<j,\qquad \chi\in\{+,-\}.$$

19289: The **residue atom** should be built as a topological boundary/corner resonance cell with two distinguished boundary poles and sign-sensitive readout. Topolectrical circuits already supply the boundary resonance and impedance-side observability directly. [Nature+1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42005-018-0035-2?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
19290: 
19291: The **root bundle** should be a small superconducting or microwave-resonator family with one mode per residue-family line. Tunable superconducting cavities and coherent microwave-bus couplings show the right control regime for that layer. [Nature+2Nature+2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-019-40891-1?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
19292: 
19293: The **quarter-turn** $J_i$ should be implemented as a calibrated coherent two-mode coupler acting on adjacent family modes. That is exactly the kind of operation microwave coherent-coupling hardware is already built to approximate. This is an engineering inference from the demonstrated coherent coupling/tunable cavity side, not a published “$J_i$-device” claim. [Nature+2Nature+2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-019-40891-1?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
19294: 
19295: The **support ledger** should not be digital software sitting outside the medium. It has to be a persistent physical register coupled slowly to the local transport layer: flux states, long-lived occupancy sectors, or another nonvolatile analog bookkeeping layer. I do not see a single demonstrated platform in the checked sources that already gives this natively together with the boundary and quarter-turn layers, which is why the hybrid route is the right answer rather than “buy platform X.” [Nature+3Nature+3Nature+3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42005-018-0035-2?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

selected_hits.snippet:
1990: A quantum circuit is an ordered sequence of gates, measurements, resets, and classical control. Measurement is an explicit instruction from a qubit to a classical bit, and even `measure_all` is just another chosen circuit operation. So readout is modeler-inserted, not canonical in the object semantics. Quantum circuits have native locality and native hidden bulk, but not intrinsic boundary semantics. They fail $B1$–$B3$. [IBM Quantum Documentation+2IBM Quantum+2](https://docs.quantum.ibm.com/api/qiskit/0.40/circuit?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
1991: 
1992: ### Open systems / structured cospans / open Petri nets
1993: 
1994: This is the closest standard comparison class. Open Petri nets explicitly designate some places as interface, and structured cospans treat systems with inputs and outputs as morphisms that compose by gluing interfaces; semantics can then be given by operational or reachability functors. But this still falls short of $B^\*$: the interface is **designated as open** by the modeler, and semantics is assigned after that opening. It is not an intrinsic boundary stratification canonically forcing a unique recursive readout in the positive-geometry sense. So this class partially matches compositional gluing, but only reaches your sharpened row **by modeling extension**, not natively. [Cambridge University Press & Assessment+1](https://www.cambridge.org/core/services/aop-cambridge-core/content/view/FCF268D95C31551004D9DFFD545F37D3/S0960129504004311a.pdf/compositional_semantics_for_open_petri_nets_based_on_deterministic_processes.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
1995: 
1996: ## Updated verdict
1997: 
1998: The first distinctness audit was too weak because it tested row 2 in a weak form. Under the sharpened row $B^\*$, the situation changes:

selected_hits.snippet:
2552: 
2553: **1) Topological-order / anyonic / TQFT-like substrates — closest current candidate, but still below Class C.**  
2554: What is native here is real: information can be stored in multi-anyon topological degeneracy, processed by braiding, and read out by fusion/measurement; boundary-bulk duality and anyon condensation give genuine access to bulk topological data through boundary excitations, including experimental protocols for extracting $R$ and $F$ data from boundary behavior. That makes this class the strongest current hit on intrinsic boundary structure and bulk/nonlocal distinction. But it still does **not** give you the positive-geometry-style object you need: no sourced unique recursive readout analogous to canonical-form residue recursion, and no clear native law that makes semantics boundary-first in the $x\to \partial x\to \Omega(x)$ sense rather than “braid/fuse then measure.” My screening call is: **G1 pass, G2 partial/pass, G3 fail/unclear, G4 partial, G5 unclear, G6 partial. Class B+, closest to C.** [PMC+3Microsoft+3ScienceDirect+3](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/?p=425721&utm_source=chatgpt.com)
2555: 
2556: **2) Open compositional rewriting substrates (structured cospans / open Petri / open graphs) — strong compositional control, but boundary is designated, not intrinsic.**  
2557: Open Petri nets explicitly designate certain places as inputs and outputs via a cospan of sets, and their semantics is then built compositionally by gluing outputs to inputs; structured cospans generalize the same idea to systems with chosen interfaces. This makes them very good fits for locality and compositional semantics, but it is exactly the wrong direction for the wedge: the boundary is **opened by the modeler** rather than arising as an intrinsic stratification of the object itself. My screening call is: **G1 pass, G2 fail, G3 fail, G4 fail, G5 fail, G6 pass. Class B.** [Cambridge University Press & Assessment+2ResearchGate+2](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/mathematical-structures-in-computer-science/article/open-petri-nets/4D1BC7F05BE8CB0C83C65F7CDF70AF2F?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
2558: 
2559: **3) Constraint-native optimization substrates (SAT/SMT, QUBO/Ising, annealers) — native admissibility, wrong semantics.**  
2560: SMT solvers natively check satisfiability of formulas and can produce models; D-Wave’s annealers natively minimize Ising/QUBO energy landscapes defined by qubit biases and couplers. That means legality/objective structure is native here. But there is no intrinsic boundary stratification, no canonical recursively boundary-determined semantics, and no Prime-style notion where the same canonical boundary/readout semantics leaves distinct bulk objects alive as a first-class feature. My screening call is: **G1 pass, G2 fail, G3 fail, G4 fail, G5 fail/unclear, G6 weak/irrelevant. Class A/B.** [Microsoft GitHub+2docs.dwavequantum.com+2](https://microsoft.github.io/z3guide/docs/optimization/intro/?utm_sourc
